summary: ResourceLoader load performance regression (compared to 3.5.3)

description.text: ### Tested versions - Reproducible in: Godot 4.4.1 - Not reproducible in: Godot 3.5.3 ### System information Godot v4.4.1.stable (9a3480ff1) - Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated NVIDIA GeForce RTX 3070 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.7283) - AMD Ryzen 9 5900X 12-Core Processor (24 threads) ### Issue description In some circumstances, `ResourceLoader.load()` and friends (`ResourceLoader.load_threaded_get()`, `@GDScript.load()`) is vastly slower than the Godot 3.5.3 counterparts. Notably, if `ResourceLoader.load()` attempts to load a scene with a script, which in turn references a different script (e.g. a global/autoload script, or a script with a `class_name`), the load time is drastically impacted. The degree of impact seems to be related to non-blank lines of code (when running from editor, commented out lines also have an effect, after export these are seemingly stripped and do not impact anything). While Godot 3.5 does seem to be slightly impacted, Godot 4.4 is massively impacted, at least 2x in our testing. This is seemingly made worse if there are multiple sub-scenes, each with a script referencing the "high-cost" script. Seemingly every scene/script pays a price of parsing the script. In terms of real world numbers/impact, our game project sees a roughly 5x increase in purely loading time, from roughly 2 seconds in Godot 3.5.3, to roughly 10 seconds in Godot 4.4.1. These numbers are using exported versions of the game. Attached are 2 minimum reproduction projects, 1 made in Godot 3.5.3, and 1 made in Godot 4.4.1. They are set up identically. ### Steps to reproduce Two projects are included: * ResourceLoader_3_5 * ResourceLoader_4_4 Both have identical setup. 1) Happy Example The `Load Scene` button loads a scene (loaded_scene_without_script.tscn) which does not have a script attached to it. 2) Sad Example The `Load Scene (with script reference)` button loads `loaded_scene_with_script.tscn`, which uses `loaded_scene_with_script.gd`. This script has a reference to `HorribleScript`, defined in `horrible_script.gd` 3) Very Sad Example The `Load Scene (with 2x script reference)` button loads `loaded_scene_with_double_script.tscn`, which uses `loaded_scene_with_double_script.gd`. This script has a reference to `HorribleScript`, defined in `horrible_script.gd`. In addition, it also includes a sub-scene `loaded_scene_with_script.tscn`, which in turn has a script referencing the same `HorribleScript` In short, both the loaded scene and one of its sub-scenes contains a reference to the same script. Note that, due to caching, only `Sad example` or `Very sad example` can be loaded in 1 session. Restart the project to compare. Note that the difference seems to be worse after exporting than when running in editor. | Godot 3.5.3 Sad | Godot 3.5.3 Very Sad | | ------------------ | ----------------------- | | | | | Godot 4.4.1 Sad | Godot 4.4.1 Very Sad | | ------------------ | ----------------------- | | | | ### Minimal reproduction project (MRP) The issue can be reproduced both by running in the editor, and/or via running an exported version. [ResourceLoader_3_5.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21672585/ResourceLoader_3_5.zip) [ResourceLoader_4_4.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21672586/ResourceLoader_4_4.zip)

summary: Modifying a CollisionShape's size affects other instances after making scene instance "Local"

description.text:
### Tested versions

Godot v4.4.1.stable

### System information

Windows 11 Pro

### Issue description

When a scene instance that contains a sub-resource (like a `CollisionShape2D`'s `shape`) is made local via the "Make Local" option, the sub-resource does not get duplicated with a new unique ID.

The problem arises because the sub-resource ID, such as `shape = SubResource("RectangleShape2D_8wb7s")`, remains identical between the newly local instance and other instances linked to the original scene file. As a result, modifying the shape's property (e.g., `size`) on the local instance through a script will also modify the shape in all other instances, as they are unintentionally sharing the same underlying resource. This breaks the expected encapsulation of a local instance.

**Proposed Solution:**
When a scene instance is made local, all its sub-resources should be recursively duplicated and assigned new, unique resource IDs to ensure they become truly independent from the original scene and other instances.

### Steps to reproduce

1.  Create a new scene (e.g., `Actor.tscn`) containing a `Node2D` as the root with this code attached
    ```gdscript
    extends Node2D
    @export var shape_size = Vector2(0,0)
    func _ready():
        # Get the shape from the instance we made local
        var local_shape = get_node("Area2D/CollisionShape2D").shape

        # Modify the size. This should only affect the local instance.
        print("Changing size of local instance's shape.")
        local_shape.size = shape_size
    ```
and `Area2D` and `CollisionShape2D` as its child.
2.  Assign a `RectangleShape2D` to the `shape` property of the `CollisionShape2D`. Save the scene.
3.  Create a new main scene (e.g., `Main.tscn`).
4.  Instance `Actor.tscn` into the main scene twice. You will have `Actor` and `Actor2` in your scene tree.
5.  Right-click on the both Actor instances in the scene tree and select **"Make Local"**.
6. Change the exported shape size for both instances differently (via the editor of course), e.g. (100,100) and (200,50).
7.  Run the main scene.
8. The both instances' shape sizes become the same size despite the different exported `shape_size`.

(I just noticed that it may be reproducible even without code. Simply trying to stretch the collision shape in the editor shows that the shapes of the two objects are moving together. This won't be fixed even after rebooting.)


### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

N/A. An MRP is not necessary as the issue is straightforward to reproduce using the steps above. Plus the bug seems to be tied to the editor's "Make Local" and Copy functionality itself, so a project file would not save much time or provide significant additional information.

summary: Default font theme override always embeds itself as sub-resource

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in: 4.5.beta [65eb6643522abbe8ebce6428fe082167a7df14f9], 4.5.dev [3bc2821e261cc1e53ac2fcfd3ad930ae35b59b17]
- Not reproducible in: 4.4.1.stable, 4.5.dev [1d8fa8b58a081b677dea42d16eb8d1361171ed71]

### System information

Godot v4.5.beta (65eb66435) - Fedora Linux 42 (Adams) on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 2 monitors - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated AMD Radeon RX 9070 XT (RADV GFX1201) - AMD Ryzen 9 9950X3D 16-Core Processor (32 threads) - 60.39 GiB memory

### Issue description

We've run into a couple of cases now where content creators will end up pushing hundreds of kilobytes (and sometimes megabytes) of font data as part of their scene files without realizing it. Upon further investigation it would seem that the thing they have in common is they all embed/sub-resource the Open Sans SemiBold font (i.e. `thirdparty/fonts/OpenSans_SemiBold.woff2`) that Godot uses as its default font, and that it's always attached to the `theme_override_fonts/font` property of something like a `Label`.

We're not totally clear on how these font properties were left at their default values, but there's a fairly straight-forward repro for this, which is to simply check the "Font" checkbox under "Theme Overrides" for any `Control` and then save the scene without actually picking a different font. This leads to the default font being embedded as a sub-resource in the scene.

This would seem to be a regression caused by #102372, which made it so that you could actually see the effective defaults for the theme overrides, and also made it so you could just check the override checkboxes without actually setting a value first. This is unlike Godot 4.4 where you had to pick a font in order to enable the font override.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Open any scene in any project.
2. Create one or more `Label`.
3. Check their "Font" checkbox under the "Theme Overrides" properties.
4. Save the scene.
5. Open the scene in a text editor.
6. Note how you have one `sub_resource type="FontFile"` for every `Label`.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

N/A

summary: Material Conversion Error Handling

description.text: Bug fixes for two issues related to Material Conversions. **Bug 1:** Select user behaviors could call a Material Conversion Plugin on a Material that has not yet initialized, throwing errors to the console, and performing a destructive conversion, deleting the original material and producing an incorrect converted result. **Steps to Recreate:** New Project, create a new CanvasItemMaterial resource, duplicate the CanvasItemMaterial, right click and "Convert to ShaderMaterial" on the duplicate resource. OR New Project, create a new CanvasItemMaterial resource, close project, reopen project, right click and "Convert to ShaderMaterial" on the CanvasItemMaterial resource. **Fix:** Expose Material.is_initialized() as a public function instead of protected so the ConversionPlugin can check initialization state to throw the error earlier in the process. The same behaviors now produce a more clear error, and don't modify/delete any files. **Bug 2:** FileSystem Dock didn't actually handle the failure of a ConversionPlugin returning a Null Ref and would crash the editor trying to use the result. **Steps to Recreate:** I couldn't find an in-editor way to produce this crash, because the Material Conversion plugins had only the most basic failure state (mat.is_null) which should never happen. If you edit a MaterialConversionPlugin to `ERR_FAIL_COND_V(true, Ref());`, build Godot, and use that conversion plugin, you can see the crash occur from FileSystemDock. **Fix:** FileSystemDock handling a convert action has a check `ERR_FAIL_COND(converted_res.is_null());`

summary: Editor freezes when calling `set_image` on an ImageTexture from an exported color setter

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in 4.5.beta5, 4.5.beta4
- Not reproducible in 4.4.1stable

### System information

Godot v4.5.beta5 - Windows 10 (build 19045) - Multi-window, 3 monitors - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - NVIDIA GeForce RTX 4090 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.6094) - AMD Ryzen 9 3900X 12-Core Processor (24 threads) - 31.94 GiB memory

### Issue description

I've created an extended class of `ImageTexture` which purpose it to let me easily create simple textures without leaving the editor. This has been working fine since 4.0, but in 4.5 the editor freezes as soon as I edit a color of the ImageTexture (See code below). I've tested exporting an int in the same way, also using `set_image(image)` but then it doesn't crash.

Something worth pointing out is that:
1. Even if it works in 4.4 without freezes, the colorpicker closes as soon as I change the color.
2. In 4.5 when instead clicking the "reset" icon the color changes but the editor doesn't freeze.
3. From my testing it seems like you must be extending the ImageTexture class with a color for it to freeze, calling `set_image` from another script that has a exported color setter doesn't trigger it.

### Steps to reproduce

Create a class like this:
```gdscript
@tool
class_name CustomTexture extends ImageTexture

var image = Image.create(50,50,false,Image.FORMAT_RGBA8)

# Editing this freezes the editor in 4.5
@export var color:Color = Color.WHITE:
	set(value):
		color = value
		set_image(image)
```
When creating a resource of this class and editing the color property the editor will freeze for what looks like indefinitely. When closing the project the "are you sure you want to close" still triggers, but other than that it's completely in-responsive.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

See class above or provided file. This project also has an int variable in the custom texture to show that that part still works.

[colorpickerfreeze.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21626443/colorpickerfreeze.zip)

summary: Allow selecting disabled items in `ItemList`

description.text:
Split off from #109352

## Disabled buttons are now selectable (if selectable toggle is on), but can't be activated.
Why: Previously, if an item was disabled, it wouldn't be selectable or clickable at all, which is inconsistent with the behaviour of regular disabled buttons, where you could select (focus) them, but not activate (press) them. This is much more useful and intuitive, because previously the selectable and disabled toggles were identical.

summary: Add font color and StyleBox customization for disabled items in ItemList

description.text:
Fixes #109348 and (partially) implements https://github.com/godotengine/godot-proposals/issues/12886

## Font Color and StyleBox Customization for Disabled Items
**Why**: A limiting issue with ItemLists was that, unlike other focusable nodes with a disabled state, there was no easy way to customize how the disabled items looked. You had no control over the styles of the item panel when it was disabled, and no easy control for the font color.

## ~~Disabled buttons are now selectable (if selectable toggle is on), but can't be activated.~~
~~Why: Previously, if an item was disabled, it wouldn't be selectable or clickable at all, which is inconsistent with the behaviour of regular disabled buttons, where you could select (focus) them, but not activate (press) them. summary: Builtin ENet implementation incompatible with external libraries

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducable in commit c81fd6c51233a727da528cf7f74137d56b5d6efe

### System information

Godot v4.5.beta (c81fd6c51) - Arch Linux - Wayland display driver - Vulkan (Forward+)

### Issue description

I would like to be able to use Godot as an ENet client with a server written using an external library (tested with the reference ENet implementation found at http://enet.bespin.org/index.html). This is not possible with the builtin library, but works if Godot is built using the external library using  the `builtin_enet=no` flag when compiling.

I find this behavior strange, as I would assume compatibility with other libraries should also be a feature in the builtin implementation. If this incompatibility is intended it would be nice to know why/how.

If this is indeed a bug I would be happy to look into it.

### Steps to reproduce

1. Build a test server based on the external library (run `make server` in the extracted archive provided, you will need to have `enet` installed, I've only tested the server on linux so this might be different on windows)
2. Run the project included in the archive under `enett`
3. Start the server using `./server`
4. Press the "connect" button in the godot game
Depending on whether the engine was built using the `builtin_enet=no` flag we either get a connection to the server or not.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

[package.zip](https://github.com/user-attachments/files/21606110/package.zip)

summary: debug window duplicating on second screen when in focus

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in: v4.4.1.stable.mono.official [49a5bc7b6] , v4.4.1.stable.official [49a5bc7b6]

### System information

Godot v4.4.1.stable.mono - Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 2 monitors - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - NVIDIA GeForce RTX 2080 (NVIDIA; 32.0.15.7700) - Intel(R) Core(TM) i5-8600K CPU @ 3.60GHz (6 threads)

### Issue description

After setting Display > Window > Viewport Width and Height to 1920 x 1080 (the resolution of both monitors):

When running a project (any scene), the debug window duplicates itself on the second monitor when the debug window is focused. You can only interact with the primary monitor (clicking ui elements, etc), but visuals for interactions appear on this secondary duplicate, making it very challenging to use.
For example, hovering over button on the primary screen: the texture change of a hover is shown on the duplicated screen and not the primary screen, but you can only toggle the button on the primary.
Interacting with the "duplicate" (clicking out of the true debug window) causes the debug window to go out of focus and the duplicate disappears. If there is another application open on the secondary monitor, you can click through this duplication as if it wasn't there.

This appears to be a visual bug involving the debug window.

Additional steps I took before this report:
- Searching for any bug already mentioned by someone else. I couldn't find a preexisting after a few hours of looking.
- Fiddling with various project and editor settings to resolve this issue (Multi-Window enabled, etc).
- Removing all Godot versions and deleting the Godot folder in %AppData to eliminate any Editor Settings that were saved. Starting again with a fresh download of the most recent Godot version.
- I've been using Godot for multiple years now, using multiple different versions. This is a _recent_ issue, but I unfortunately cannot pinpoint when it began.

Apologies if the video quality is not the best, had to use a phone.

https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/2e3a9a4c-36c6-4237-93e6-28d48040b3bc

### Steps to reproduce

With a fresh download of the latest version Godot v4.4.1.stable or mono, and default project and editor settings:
1. Set Display > Window > Viewport Width and Height to 1920 x 1080 (match primary monitor resolution).
2. Make any basic scene, no scripts. Example scene: TextureRect in the center with variable size.
3. Run scene.
4. Debug window focuses, duplicate appears on secondary monitor.
5. Click anywhere on secondary monitor, debug goes out of focus and duplicate disappears.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

**Reproduction steps appear to not be project dependent**, but just in case there is a setting I am not aware of:

summary: Basis Universal texture compression causes "null function or function signature mismatch" error in HTML5 exports

description.text:
### Tested versions

### Issue description

When exporting a project to HTML5, when it tries to load a texture with Basis Universal compression, it causes a "null function or function signature mismatch" error to display. In the browser console, the following appears:

    at 0c665d2e:0x9c7e6d

summary: Add Anisotropic Filtering and Wrap Options to gltf Reimport.

description.text:

Relevant fixes are cherry-picked for stable branches as needed by maintainers.

summary: Incorrect rendering of textures, materials, or scenes

description.text:
### Tested versions

v4.6.dev4.official [bd2ca13c6] v4.6.dev3.official [9d84f3d13] v4.6.dev2.official [7864ac8] v4.6.dev1.official [8d8041b] v4.5.stable.official [876b290], v4.5.beta6.official [d5ad055], v4.5.beta5.official [c81fd6c], v4.5.beta4.official [2d113cc], v4.4.1.stable.official [49a5bc7]

### System information

arch, linux-x86-64, x11

### Issue description

These are three issues that I believe are interconnected:

1. I'm not sure if this is intended, but initially, scenes do not have their screenshots. Only after saving a scene does its screenshot appear. This breaks the logic, as the image might not accurately represent the actual scene. Sometimes this results in a visual glitch. In the video below, I wasn’t able to capture this, but previously it has happened that the screenshot comes from a different scene. Possibly this mismatch effect is the root cause.

2. Textures do not always match the actual ones. This was especially noticeable with the .ktx format before. But in the video, I’m showing an example with Texture2DArray, which looks correct in some cases and incorrect in others. However, as far as I understand, this is a general rendering issue and not directly caused by Texture2DArray.

3. When importing, for example, .glb models, their material appears incorrect. You can see that it looks like it's loaded in blocky patterns, which doesn’t happen when loading the texture separately. However, recently in version v4.5.beta4, the texture initially loads as visually white, and only after creating a unique version of it does it become incorrectly rendered.

All three of these are visual issues for me and seem to be related to a broader problem with incorrect rendering.

### Steps to reproduce

https://youtu.be/sVUIh-LQ-mE

The video sequentially demonstrates all three of the issues described above.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

I can't share the project, as it is under closed development. However, I want to note that this is a common practice across my other projects as well. That’s why I associate this behavior specifically with how the engine currently functions.

I would like this to be acknowledged as an existing rendering issue, with the hope it may be addressed in future updates after further testing. I also understand that the issue may be broken down into subtopics if you consider them unrelated or if micro-management is needed.

description.text:

arch, linux-x86-64, x11

### Issue description

I encountered this issue after switching from v4.5.beta3.official [4d1f26e] to beta4. At first, the collision just started disappearing — I assumed it was a bug but couldn’t link it to anything specific. Eventually, I figured out that it happens after switching scenes. I can’t say for sure whether this is an engine bug or a project-level issue at this point. But it seems likely that, once again, a missing boolean flag in the engine is causing the collision to be invisible — since other collisions still work as expected.

***
There are also other inconsistencies — like scene thumbnails not matching reality, or a texture in the scene tree not being what it actually is in the scene, or materials not corresponding to their real appearance. I’m not sure if I should create a separate issue for these?
***

summary: Flatpak Godots cannot load project from another partition

description.text:
### Tested versions

- Reproducible in flatpak Godots v1.3.stable, Godot v4.x
- NOT Reproducible in AUR Godots
- NOT Reproducible with direct install of Godot from flatpak
- NOT Reproducible with direct install of Godot from https://godotengine.org/download/linux/

### System information

EndeavourOS, Godot v4.4.1.stable - Freedesktop SDK 24.08 (Flatpak runtime) on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 2 monitors - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - AMD Radeon 780M (radeonsi, phoenix, LLVM 19.1.7, DRM 3.63, 6.15.4-arch2-1) - AMD Ryzen 7 7840HS w/ Radeon 780M Graphics (16 threads)

### Issue description

Summary:
The flatpak version of Godots changes from the selected path for my `project.godot` file to a temp copy in `/run/user/1000`, but the rest of the project remains in its original directory. This causes Godot to fail to load the rest of the project, because it only looks in the temp file directory.

Caveats:
I could only reproduce the issue when combining all following factors:
* Using Godots instead of using installing Godot directly
* Using *flatpak* version of Godots
* Project stored in different partition from OS root and flatpak

I also have my partitions on LVM instead of physical partitions, but I don't expect that to factor in.

Hypotheses:
My understanding of flatpak apps is that they're sandboxed such that when an app wants access to a file it has to be mounted as a temp file to give access to the sandbox. My guess is that the file selector dialog for importing a `project.godot` file *only* causes flatpak to import the one file.

Is this something that I can resolve with just some configuration changes? To flatpak? To Godots?

### Steps to reproduce

Steps:
1. Install flatpak Godots from flathub (tested: `flatpak` CLI and bauh)
2. Install your editor (tested: v4.3, 4.4, 4.4.1)
3. Import your `project.godot` file from another partition
4. Note that the `Project Path` field now contains a path to a temp file; e.g. `/run/user/1000/doc/ad393931/project.godot`.
5. Finish the import and edit the project
6. Note the error messages in the output panel. E.g. `ERROR: Cannot save project metadata to file 'res://.godot/editor/project_metadata.cfg'.`

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

I can reproduce the same issue in any version of Godot / Godots
1. Create new project on another partition
2. Create and save new scene
3. Set scene as main scene in `Project > Project Settings > General > Application > Run > Main Scene`
4. Save and re-open project
5. Note the error dialog that says `Error loading scene, it must be inside the project path. Use 'import' to open the scene, then save it inside the project path.`

summary: Write directories to export preset's export_files when entire folder is selected

description.text: This PR helps declutter `export_presets.cfg` by replacing individual files with directories in `export_files` entries where relevant (when all files in a directory are selected). Updating the selection in the editor's export dialog automatically switches from files to directories or from directories to files as needed. As a nice side-effect, this also means that when a folder is added to `export_files`, creating new files (or subfolders) in that folder will automatically include them, instead of having to manually update affected presets. > [!important] > This changes the current behavior of the export logic, with regards to later additions to the filesystem. > > The current master branch explicitly lists every file that is selected in the tree, which has the following implications: > - The `export_files` lists is exhaustive, what is found in the list is what will be included/excluded during export. > - Any addition to the filesystem (new files, with or without subfolders) is ignored, and export templates must be updated accordingly. > > On the other hand, this PR behaves as follows: > - The `export_files` list is not exhaustive from a file point of view, but listed directories include all subdirectories and files within. > - When files are added to the filesystem (with or without subfolders), in a directory that is listed in `export_files` (or subdirectory thereof), those files are implicitly selected as well, so templates that include/exclude folders will automatically add the new subfolders/files in those folders. > - Of course, if you deselect a file inside a previously selected folder, `export_files` is updated to reflect the changes. > > The main implication is that the behavior switches from "never update the list that was explicitly defined at one point in time" to "select anything that is inside an already selected directory". Here, "selected directory" means that everything in the directory is selected, and any later addition to that directory is assumed to be included as well. > > I can acknowledge that this change may confuse some users, but I also believe that the current behavior is a bigger issue, as every single time you create a new file inside a directory that you previously selected, you *have to* add that file to the template, and you can see the previously selected folder is now in a partially-selected state instead (my use case being an entire folder containing files to be excluded from export (as those will be exported as separate PCK files), but new files keep creeping in if I forget to update the template). Closes godotengine/godot-proposals#12760. For illustration purposes, an export preset in one of my projects looks like the following: In the current master branch, this results in 696 entries in `export_files`, which results in a 45,499-character line; with this PR, we only have 8 entries, and a 262-character line. ``` master: export_files=PackedStringArray("res://addons/gdscript_xml_converter/bin/convert_xml.gd", "res://addons/gdscript_xml_converter/src/class_definition/annotation_def.gd", [...693 other files...], "res://modules/module_tab.tscn") This PR: export_files=PackedStringArray("res://addons/gdscript_xml_converter/", "res://addons/gdUnit4/", "res://addons/godot_color_maps/", "res://addons/godot_insim/demo/", "res://addons/godot_insim/test/", "res://docs_generator/", "res://experiments/", "res://modules/") ``` For both "export selected resources" and "export all resources in the project except resources checked below", exported PCK files are identical to current master (if there are unit tests for exporting, let me know). > [!note] > Opening the export dialog with this PR will automatically update the `export_presets.cfg` file to use the new format; going back to master will remove all folders from `export_files` entries, which will likely result in missing files in the affected export templates.

summary: POT generation for tooltips ignores `tooltip_auto_translate_mode`

description.text:
### Tested versions

Reproducible in:
- 4.5.dev [45509c284]
- 4.4.1.stable
- 4.3.stable

### System information

Godot v4.5.beta (c2202d36c) - Windows 11 (build 26100) - Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - integrated AMD Radeon 780M Graphics (Advanced Micro Devices, Inc.; 32.0.12019.1028) - AMD Ryzen 7 8840HS w/ Radeon 780M Graphics (16 threads) - 15.28 GiB memory

### Issue description

POT generation ignores the auto translate mode for tooltips (and possibly other cases, not using other cases so not sure what other areas are affected), this means that tooltips with `Always` selected but `Disable` selected on the control itself are ignored, and tooltips with `Disable` selected on controls with `Always` are included

This seems to be due to a overly simple check in the generator:
https://github.com/godotengine/godot/blob/45509c284cc8779f7e791b0d7dc3d639b3cc2fcc/editor/translations/packed_scene_translation_parser_plugin.cpp#L91-L99

### Steps to reproduce

- Open the MRP
- Open the project settings, go to `Localization > POT Generator`
- Generate a new *separate* POT file from the existing `Out.pot` file
- Check that it is missing:
  - "Should Appear Tooltip B"
  - "Should Appear Tooltip C1"
- Check that it has the following incorrect entries:
  - "Should Not Appear Tooltip A"
  - "Should Not Appear Tooltip B1"

Note when running the project that the tooltips and texts are correctly translated (displayed using pseudolocalization) so this is strictly an issue with the POT generator

As a workaround you can use the technique used in `Main.gd` which is how "Should Appear Tooltip C2" is correctly extracted:
```gdscript
$VBoxContainer/ButtonE.tooltip_text = "Should Appear Tooltip C2"
```

You can similarly prevent it with the trick for "Should Not Appear Tooltip B2" using `# NO_TRANSLATE`:
```gdscript
$VBoxContainer/ButtonC.tooltip_text = "Should Not Appear Tooltip B2" # NO_TRANSLATE
```

The workarounds are pretty trivial but for larger projects with cases where text shouldn't be translated it can grow large, especially having to separate where things are declared

summary: Ctrl key shortcuts in ItemList are interpreted as search

description.text:
### Tested versions

Encountered in v4.4.1, reproduced in v4.5.beta [c2202d36c]

### System information

Godot v4.5.beta (c2202d36c) - Fedora Linux 41 (Workstation Edition) on Wayland - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 2 monitors - OpenGL 3 (Compatibility) - AMD Radeon RX 6750 XT (radeonsi, navi22, LLVM 19.1.7, DRM 3.63, 6.15.7-100.fc41.x86_64) - AMD Ryzen 9 7900X 12-Core Processor (24 threads) - 61.90 GiB memory

### Issue description

When using ctrl key shortcuts in the editor with an ItemList control focused, if the control has the "Allow Search" flag set, the key is interpreted as a search request, and if there is a matching item then it is selected and the relevant signals are emitted. For example, pressing ctrl-s will switch the selection to the first item starting with s, if there is one. I would expect key input to be ignored in this situation if a modifier is pressed.

Subsequent ctrl key combinations do not necessarily behave the same way until the focus is changed or the control is interacted with in some other way.

I noticed this in a custom editor plugin that had a screen similar to the editor's built in script screen. However, the problem also occurs in ItemLists in the editor, such as those in the script editor.

### Steps to reproduce

To reproduce in the editor:

1. Create a script and add several methods starting with different letters.
2. Make sure the methods list has focus and select one of the items.
3. Press the ctrl key and one of the letters that one of the other methods starts with.
4. Note that the editor jumps to the method starting with that letter.

The attached project includes a plugin with a main screen editor that has an ItemList that switches between different containers, with labels identifying the selected list item. To reproduce:

1. Open the "ItemList Example" screen.
2. Select one of the items in the list (e.g. "First item").
3. Press ctrl and a letter key that one of the other items begins with (e.g. ctrl-s)
4. Note that the selection switches to the item starting with that letter (e.g. "Second Item")

summary: Documentation: warn about NavigationAgent3D emitting `velocity_computed` when processing is disabled

description.text:
### Tested versions

Reproducible in 4.4.1 stable

### System information

Godot v4.4.1.stable (49a5bc7b6) - Freedesktop SDK 24.08 (Flatpak runtime) on X11 - X11 display driver, Multi-window, 1 monitor - Vulkan (Forward+) - dedicated AMD Radeon RX 6600 (RADV NAVI23) - AMD Ryzen 5 5600 6-Core Processor (12 threads)

### Issue description

NavigationAgent3D emits `velocity_computed` when the entire node processing (`process_mode`) is set to disabled in the editor. Note that avoidance has to be enabled in order for this signal to emit - this is a normal part of the feature.

I have seen #81761 and I understand why this is the case, but when a scene root is disabled, the default behavior of bodies is to remove them (CollisionObject3D > Disable Mode), which then causes issues with velocity processing, such as `move_and_slide()` reporting errors each physics frame due to not having a body to move (the solution to this is to change that body setting).

I think the expected behavior would be to stop emitting that signal when the node isn't processing.

If similarly to #81761 this is contrary to the navigation system architecture, I think it would be good to at least warn about this behavior in the docs; the current description doesn't really underline it:

> velocity_computed(safe_velocity: [Vector3](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/classes/class_vector3.html#class-vector3)) [🔗](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/classes/class_navigationagent3d.html#class-navigationagent3d-signal-velocity-computed)
> Notifies when the collision avoidance velocity is calculated. Emitted every update as long as [avoidance_enabled](https://docs.godotengine.org/en/stable/classes/class_navigationagent3d.html#class-navigationagent3d-property-avoidance-enabled) is true and the agent has a navigation map.

### Steps to reproduce

Set up a basic NavigationAgent3D scene, this can be a Node3D with the agent as a child. Enable avoidance, connect the `velocity_computed` signal to a script function and make it print something. Disable the NavAgent processing (Node > Process > Mode > Disabled). Run and see that the text is being printed.

### Minimal reproduction project (MRP)

Save this as a `.tscn` file and run it (F6): 

```
[gd_scene load_steps=2 format=3 uid="uid://brkolq03b2xd1"]

[sub_resource type="GDScript" id="GDScript_jrjqt"]
script/source = "extends NavigationAgent3D


func _on_velocity_computed(safe_velocity: Vector3) -> void:
	print(\"hello world\")
"

[node name="Node3D" type="Node3D"]

[node name="Agent" type="NavigationAgent3D" parent="."]
process_mode = 4
avoidance_enabled = true
script = SubResource("GDScript_jrjqt")

[connection signal="velocity_computed" from="Agent" to="Agent" method="_on_velocity_computed"]
```
